name: Open/Closed Principle
source: Clean Code by Robert C. Martin, Chapter 10: Classes
problem: Adding new behavior requires modifying existing, working classes, risking regressions in tested code and forcing the same classes to be changed repeatedly for every new requirement.
steps: Design classes to be open for extension (new behavior can be added) but closed for modification (existing code is not changed).
Introduce abstractions (interfaces or abstract classes) where variation is expected.
Add new behavior by creating new classes that implement or extend the abstraction.
Use dependency injection to supply the appropriate implementation at runtime.
Protect stable code behind the abstraction; only the new subclass is modified or added.
